text: A two-centimeter incision is made at the umbilicus of the abdomen, accessed using a tripod setup for a single incision cholecystectomy.

The surgeon uses a 5mm grasper to retract the gallbladder superiorly. A two-row silk suture on a straight needle is passed through the infundibulum of the gallbladder and out laterally, enabling manipulation of the gallbladder neck for better exposure.

The surgeon uses a conventional straight hook dissector to incise the peritoneum over the neck of the gallbladder, exposing the cystic duct and artery. Posterior peritoneal attachments are similarly incised. The cystic duct and cystic duct node are clearly visualized, and the cystic artery is dissected free. Five millimeter locking hemo clips are applied across the cystic duct, which is then divided.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect the remaining attachments between the gallbladder and the liver bed in a retrograde fashion. Medial and lateral retraction of the silk suture provides excellent exposure, allowing the gallbladder to be freed from the liver bed.

The surgeon irrigates the surgical site before grasping the gallbladder and withdrawing it through the umbilical incision. The fascial defect at the umbilicus is closed with interrupted zero PDS suture, followed by closure of the skin with fine absorbable suture.
